findings.title: Practice profiles encode a detailed institutional contracting playbook and approval ontology

evidence.description: The shared practice profile distinguishes sales and purchasing sides and directs every review to use risk appetite, standard/fallback terms, thresholds, and deal positions.
The sales-side schema records cap components, carveouts, fallbacks, never-accept positions, data-protection terms, governing-law positions, and a deal-breaker.
The profile records approval levels, escalation recipients, channels, dollar thresholds, and automatic escalation triggers.
The skill operationalizes the stored matrix to name an approver and identify automatic triggers.

source_to_sink: Attorney/team answers and maintained playbook
Plain-text practice profile under ~/.claude/plugins/config/claude-for-legal
Contract-review and escalation skills read matching side and thresholds
Review or draft escalation output
findings.proves: The source is designed to capture and repeatedly apply firm-specific contracting preferences, forbidden outcomes, fallback positions, and decision authority.
findings.does_not_prove: It does not prove provider-side retention, training, cross-customer pooling, ownership transfer, or that a populated profile ever left customer-controlled storage.
findings.alternative_explanation: This is the intended benign mechanism for customer-specific adaptation and consistent work product.
findings.test_to_confirm: Inspect a populated profile's filesystem custody, model-request payloads, access logs, tenant controls, retention terms, and deletion behavior.

findings.title: Product-legal onboarding deliberately derives calibration from prior attorney decisions

evidence.description: The onboarding flow requests ten past launch reviews, builds a blocked-versus-shipped calibration table, and writes the profile.
The skill says it learns risk calibration and house framework, writes a persistent plain-text profile, and disclaims reading personal history or memory.
It explicitly asks to read past legal reviews because that is where calibration resides.
The flow extracts what was raised, blocked, or waved through into a reusable risk-calibration table.

source_to_sink: Past launch-review documents and user-approved interview answers
Model reads and extracts decision patterns
Persistent product-legal CLAUDE.md calibration table
Future launch-review commands reuse the profile
findings.proves: The design intentionally converts past professional decisions into reusable operating policy, a concrete form of workflow and judgment capture.
findings.does_not_prove: Inference-time reading and local persistence do not establish generalized training, provider retention, or use for another customer.
findings.alternative_explanation: The extraction exists to reduce repetitive onboarding and reproduce the customer's own standards within its future requests.
findings.test_to_confirm: Capture onboarding network traffic and resulting files; verify whether source reviews or extracted calibration are retained server-side and under what contractual purpose limitation.

findings.title: Matter workspaces persist client context and history locally with explicit cross-matter controls but archive rather than delete

evidence.description: New matters create matter, history, and notes files; closure archives them; cross-matter reads require the configured flag.
Matter intake records confidentiality, facts, stakes, playbook overrides, and related matters, then persists files.
Cross-matter context defaults off and requires an explicit user request when on; closure is not deletion and retention policy is out of scope.
The IP implementation repeats default isolation and explicitly notes archived retention.

source_to_sink: Client/matter intake and later events
Local matter.md/history.md/notes.md files
Active-matter selection gates skill reads
Archived folder remains readable for retention/conflicts
findings.proves: The repository specifies a persistent, customer-side matter memory with a default confidentiality boundary and an acknowledged retention gap.
findings.does_not_prove: Prompt-level instructions alone do not prove runtime enforcement, secure deletion, filesystem ACL isolation, encryption, or provider inability to observe inference payloads.
findings.alternative_explanation: Archival supports legitimate conflicts, continuity, and legal-retention needs while default-off cross-matter access mitigates accidental mixing.
findings.test_to_confirm: Attempt cross-matter reads under every flag/confidentiality combination; inspect filesystem permissions, request traces, archive deletion, legal-hold, and retention controls.

findings.title: Scheduled agents can read legal systems and post derived operational signals through remote connectors

evidence.description: A scheduled renewal agent has Read, Write, Ironclad wildcard, and Slack-send tools.
It reads configuration, refreshes from a connected CLM, and posts reports to a configured destination.
The docket agent reads portfolio and matter history, pulls remote docket entries, writes reports and matter history, and posts a Slack summary.
The regulatory monitor runs on a configured cadence, reads firm watchlists/materiality thresholds, pulls feeds, runs policy diffs, and posts a digest.

source_to_sink: CLM, docket, feed, practice-profile, and matter data
Scheduled agent reads and classifies
Local output/history plus Slack message
Configured external systems and workspace recipients
findings.proves: The static design includes ambient workflows that transmit selected derived legal-operational information to configured external destinations.
findings.does_not_prove: It does not show an actual deployment, actual payloads, provider retention, unauthorized access, covert exfiltration, or training use.
findings.alternative_explanation: The outbound paths are explicit user-configured workflow functions—alerts and reports—not concealed collection channels.
findings.test_to_confirm: Deploy in an instrumented tenant with canary records; inspect scheduler identity, connector scopes, exact Slack payloads, approval controls, audit logs, and retry/dead-letter retention.

findings.title: Plugin-level connector declarations and wildcard tool grants create broad trust boundaries

evidence.description: The plugin declares remote Slack and Google Drive MCP endpoints described as able to search/read workspace messages and documents.
The external plugin declares a Thomson Reuters HTTP MCP endpoint and OAuth client ID.
The agent grants wildcard Trellis, CourtListener, and Slack-send tool names.
The monitor grants WebFetch and wildcard Slack-send authority.

source_to_sink: Workspace messages, documents, legal research, and docket sources
Remote MCP/tool invocation under runtime-granted credentials
Agent/model context and derived output
Configured connector operator or Slack workspace
findings.proves: The repository declares remote integrations and some broad wildcard capability surfaces whose effective scope depends on runtime authorization.
findings.does_not_prove: Declarations do not establish granted OAuth scopes, calls made, data returned, destination retention, malicious intent, or secondary use.
findings.alternative_explanation: Wildcard names may be a concise static declaration while the runtime, OAuth consent, and connector enforce least privilege.
findings.test_to_confirm: Record OAuth scopes, tool discovery, per-call authorization, connector audit events, tenant boundaries, revocation, and destination retention in a test deployment.

findings.title: Internal-investigation workflow builds a highly sensitive evidentiary corpus and derived findings

evidence.description: The workflow creates a persistent investigation log, sources checklist, and document-review ledger with parties, allegation, issues, gaps, and metadata.
The checklist covers interviews, email/messages, HR history, prior complaints, comparator data, policies, org charts, calendars, and warning records.
The resulting memo contains factual findings and issue-level conclusions under a stated evidentiary standard.
The skill disclaims making disciplinary decisions or guaranteeing privilege.

source_to_sink: Allegations, interviews, communications, personnel and audit records
Local investigation logs and document ledger
Model-supported synthesis
Internal memo, HR summary, or attorney-reviewed external-response workflow
findings.proves: The design can organize sensitive facts and transform them into reusable factual findings and an investigation record.
findings.does_not_prove: It does not prove any investigation was run, data was remotely retained, privilege attaches, disciplinary decisions were automated, or the data trained a model.
findings.alternative_explanation: The corpus and audit trail support defensible, attorney-directed investigations and source completeness.
findings.test_to_confirm: Use synthetic investigation data to inspect storage ACLs, encryption, model payloads, privilege-circle access, exports, deletion, support access, and retention.

findings.title: Managed-agent manifests demonstrate meaningful least-privilege and prompt-injection controls

evidence.description: The orchestrator disables tools by default, enables only local reads, and delegates to bounded subagents; the prompt requires untrusted feed treatment.
The feed reader treats instructions as data, refuses exfiltration behavior, restricts fetches to allowlisted hosts, and blocks body-supplied URLs.
Tool-layer allowed hosts, redirect limits, private-network blocking, no MCP, and no write capability enforce parts of the boundary.
Untrusted contract instructions are treated as data and the reader is explicitly read-only with schema-constrained output.

source_to_sink: Untrusted feed or contract content
Read-only leaf with schema and tool restrictions
Validated structured handoff
Separate computation/writer stage
findings.proves: Some managed-agent variants encode defense in depth: disabled-by-default tools, leaf-level write isolation, schema constraints, egress allowlists, and prompt-injection instructions.
findings.does_not_prove: Static manifests do not prove the runtime honors every configuration, that all agents use these hardened cookbooks, or that allowed destinations are harmless.
findings.alternative_explanation: This is affirmative security architecture consistent with H0 and weighs against a claim of indiscriminate data access.
findings.test_to_confirm: Run adversarial feed/contract fixtures and verify tool denials, schema rejection, redirect handling, private-network blocking, and absence of writer/MCP capability at runtime.

findings.title: Human approval and proposal-only gates constrain consequential policy and legal actions

evidence.description: A redraft is written as a separate proposal and cannot close the gap until a policy owner applies and approves it.
The workflow refuses direct source-policy edits and requires current-version confirmation.
The skill names the approver and drafts the ask but explicitly does not send it.
A communication-log entry captures detailed contact facts but must be shown to and confirmed by the user before writing.

source_to_sink: Gap, contract issue, or communication facts
Draft/proposed artifact
Human review and approval gate
Separate human-controlled application, sending, or confirmed write
findings.proves: Multiple workflows reserve consequential acts for a human and distinguish a model-generated proposal from an approved state change.
findings.does_not_prove: Prompt instructions alone do not guarantee runtime compliance, prevent all mistaken writes, or govern provider-side data handling.
findings.alternative_explanation: These controls are central product safety requirements rather than incidental disclaimers.
findings.test_to_confirm: Attempt direct apply/send/write requests and verify deterministic refusal, user confirmation receipts, immutable audit events, and permission-layer enforcement.

new_patterns.name: Decision-to-policy calibration
new_patterns.logic: Locate workflows that read prior outcomes or user answers, classify blocked/accepted/deviated decisions, and persist the abstraction for later reuse.
new_patterns.false_positive_risk: Documentation may describe an optional onboarding path that was never executed; persistence does not imply provider retention.

new_patterns.name: Ambient connector handoff
new_patterns.logic: Correlate schedule/cadence, remote read capability, local transformation, and outbound post/write capability across agent and managed-agent manifests.
new_patterns.false_positive_risk: Static capabilities may be disabled or narrowed by deployment-time scopes and may never be invoked.

new_patterns.name: Retention without deletion
new_patterns.logic: Find close/archive operations that preserve readable matter histories while declaring retention policy out of scope.
search_terms: retention policy is out of scope
new_patterns.false_positive_risk: Retention may be legally required and controlled by external storage policy not represented in the repository.

new_patterns.name: Runtime-enforced leaf isolation
new_patterns.logic: Identify disabled-by-default tools, no-write/no-MCP leaves, output schemas, allowed-host controls, and private-network blocks as counterevidence to unrestricted-agent theories.
new_patterns.false_positive_risk: Manifest syntax may not be enforced by the actual hosted runtime or may differ from deployed configuration.

negative_evidence.description: Lines 94 and 100 state that setup does not read personal Claude history, other conversations, home-directory CLAUDE.md, or other personal memory, and only folds in typed or approved inputs.
negative_evidence.effect: Weighs against an interpretation that the static onboarding design silently mines unrelated conversation history.

negative_evidence.description: Lines 175-179 make cross-matter reading default-off and require an explicit user request even when enabled.
negative_evidence.effect: Weighs against an interpretation that cross-client context is automatically pooled by the workspace logic.

negative_evidence.description: Lines 22-58 impose host allowlisting, private-network blocking, redirect limits, read-only operation, no MCP, and schema-only output.
negative_evidence.effect: Provides concrete static security controls against arbitrary egress and prompt-injection-driven tool use.

negative_evidence.description: Lines 25-39 grant only local read/grep/glob and explicitly deny MCP, web, write, and further agents.
negative_evidence.effect: Shows architectural compartmentalization rather than every stage receiving broad external authority.

negative_evidence.description: Lines 35-38 prohibit source-policy mutation and reserve application/approval for the policy owner.
negative_evidence.effect: Weighs against claims that the static workflow autonomously changes approved institutional policy.

negative_evidence.description: Lines 63-70 disclaim calendaring, require lawyer verification, distrust heuristic classification, avoid closed matters, and preserve the docketing system's role.
negative_evidence.effect: Shows bounded automation and human verification for malpractice-sensitive outputs.

unknowns.question: Does the hosted Claude runtime retain or reuse populated profiles, matter contents, past reviews, tool results, or derived calibrations beyond the requested service?
unknowns.why_source_cannot_answer: The pinned repository describes prompts, local paths, and static integrations, not hosted inference logging, support access, retention, deletion, or training systems.
unknowns.needed_evidence: Authenticated contract/DPA terms, tenant settings, runtime request traces, retention/deletion logs, support-access logs, and provider training/product-use controls.

unknowns.question: What OAuth scopes and per-tool permissions are actually granted to Slack, Drive, Ironclad, iManage, Trellis, CourtListener, and CoCounsel connectors?
unknowns.why_source_cannot_answer: Static MCP declarations and wildcard tool names do not expose consented scopes, tenant configuration, server implementation, or runtime authorization decisions.
unknowns.needed_evidence: OAuth consent records, discovered tool schemas, connector administration settings, access logs, token audience/scope data, and revocation tests.

unknowns.question: Are prompt-level matter isolation, human approval, and no-write instructions deterministically enforced?
unknowns.why_source_cannot_answer: The files specify desired behavior but do not include execution receipts or complete platform enforcement code.
unknowns.needed_evidence: Adversarial runtime tests, tool-call traces, filesystem ACL tests, policy-engine configuration, confirmation receipts, and failed-action audit logs.

unknowns.question: Are scheduled agents deployed, by whom, and what exact records do they send to external destinations?
unknowns.why_source_cannot_answer: Agent prompts and manifests express capability and intended cadence but contain no deployment state or observed payloads.
unknowns.needed_evidence: Deployment manifests, scheduler inventory, service identities, run logs, outbound payload captures, channel membership, retry queues, and deletion policies.

unknowns.question: Can any firm-specific calibration learned in one tenant improve another tenant's product behavior?
unknowns.why_source_cannot_answer: No assigned static file contains evidence of cross-customer aggregation, generalized training, federated learning, product analytics reuse, or tenant-to-tenant retrieval.
unknowns.needed_evidence: Provider architecture, data-lineage records, training/evaluation dataset provenance, feature analytics specifications, tenant-isolation tests, and contractual purpose limitations.

unknowns.question: How are archived matters and sensitive investigation files encrypted, expired, placed on hold, exported, and securely deleted?
unknowns.why_source_cannot_answer: Matter-workspace text explicitly says retention policy is out of scope and the investigation instructions describe logical paths rather than storage controls.
unknowns.needed_evidence: Storage architecture, encryption/key controls, ACLs, retention schedules, legal-hold configuration, backup lifecycle, export audit logs, and deletion verification.
